segments.text: Over the next couple of years.
Awesome. So I understand we've got the, uh, chief sensor
and deputy chief sensor with us.
Welcome very much, um, in those capacities
to the governance and Administration Committee.
We've got you here until, I think, uh, for,
we're just starting a minute early, about half an hour, um,
to hear from you, uh, around, uh, your annual view.
So we'll, um, open up to you if you would like
to make a few comments to the committee,
and then we'll move to, uh, questions from members.
So, welcome and over to you, Carol.
Thank you. Um, well, I'm Caroline Flore. I'm the Chief
Rupert ABT Hampton Deputy Chief Sensor.
Um, so we are both statutory appointments under
the Classification Act.
Um, we discharge functions under that act.
We serve as Chief and Deputy Chief Executive,
and we make up the board of the office,
and we are both serving our second terms.
Um, we've met with you as a committee
before, I think you broadly understand what we do.
Um, but I do have some, um, opening remarks,
but if you do need any clarification on our
functions, please let me know.
Um, so in the annual review period, we had a very busy year
and a lot to be proud of.
Um, firstly, after several consecutive years
of PO posting a financial deficit, the board
and the management team have turned this around.
Through careful management
and the implementation of an investment
and sustainability approach, we returned a surplus
of 138,000 against a projected deficit of 62,000.
Over the last three years, we've followed a simple
and clear strategy focusing on doing our core business well.
We've made prudence spending decisions
and we've reviewed our operating model
to modernize the delivery of our statutory functions.
We've been acutely focused on maximizing the impact
of the classifications we make
because they do have significant public
safety ramifications.
This has involved work to make our functions as efficient
and effective as possible
to represent good value for public money.
And we're in a position today where we are about
to procure new technology to really amplify the benefit
of our classification work.
And this has only been possible
after three years of type financial management
to replenish our cash reserves.
We work under 30-year-old legislation
that was designed in an analog era well before the internet.
Our functions were originally envisaged to ensure
that movies and other entertainment media were correctly
labeled, and that objectionable content found as part
of criminal investigations could be classified
for legal certainty.
The world has changed, and so have we.
We don't have a mandate to regulate the internet,
but by taking a public health approach
to the mandate we do have,
we are delivering tangible benefits.
The New Zealand public, the core elements
of the classification act
that we work under have stood the test of time.
And while it's not feasible to think
that we could classify every piece of content
before it's seen, we can equip people with what they need
to keep themselves and their families safe.
Um, we can also respond to the worst of the worst content
to help our colleagues in the wider system
take SWIFT investigations, potentially get it removed online
and hold offenders accountable.
So we've taken the time to review
and refine our operating model
by doing work efficiently and to a high standard.
It means we've been able to focus our very skilled
and experienced staff on the growing demand
for our forensic work.
So that is the work that's potentially, uh, going
to result in a classification of objectionable.
We're investing in new tech to support our team
and to make our services more accessible to law enforcement.
And to give you a sense of how our changes are working.
In the annual review period, 24 25,
we classified 856 forensic publications.
And as of Monday this week,
we have already classified over 1500
forensic classifications just seven months into the year.
And this has been, uh, as a result
of very careful implementation of our operating model.
Community leaders we engage with.
And they have told us that they're concerned about the
content that the young people are seeing online, not only
because of the harm that it can do, but
'cause they're largely unaware that
that extreme content might also be illegal.
Um, and it's, it is our role in legislation
to educate the public about the classification system.
So we shared their concern.
Um, we see this as basic civics education.
If people are unaware of their rights
and responsibilities under the law, then we need
to do a better job of telling them.
So we went around the country talking
with young people about their experiences
with extreme content online to inform our approach,
and we highlighted their voices in a
report that we are really proud of.
We released last year called content that Crosses the Line.
Young people told us that it's not an uncommon experience
to come across extremely harmful content,
even if they weren't looking for it.
It appears in social media, but also in group, group chats
or maybe sent to them directly.
And they were very candid with us.
Young people today are just as curious as the generations
before them, which is a natural part of growing up.
Um, but today their curiosity may lead them directly
to extremely harmful content,
and that leaves lasting impression.
They asked for supportive nonjudgmental conversations
with the adults in their lives about what they're seeing.
And so we've responded with resources, information
for parents, and a professional development training
for people who work with young people.
We recently followed up this research with a national survey
of adults and their experiences online.
And the results were broadly similar
with adults showing a mixed understanding of the law,
but with deep concern about the content.
So we've responded with explicit information about the types
of content that could be objectionable
and the actions people can take, including reporting it.
We can't be queasy about this issue anymore.
We do our best to take a modern reasoned approach
to modern complex demands within a very small budget
and small team.
We've enjoyed a constructive relationship
with the Department of Internal Affairs,
who are our monitoring agency,
but are also, uh, the investigative
and enforcement arm of the Classification Act.
And they lead policy work to improve our regulatory system.
So we are very proud of the work that we do
to keep Kiwi safe from harm.
And we are happy to talk to you through any
of it in more detail.
Thank you so much for those, uh, comments.
And we do have some questions from members.
We'll start with Reen. David.
Thank you. And yeah, thanks for those open comments.
Excellent. Same set for us.
Um, in, in the report you talk about
focusing resources on areas where there is the most harm.
So, um, I guess as an opening question on that, I'm keen
to know if you can provide some more detail about
where you see the most harm currently.
So, um, we have, um, a fixed, uh, crown funding.
And we have a small amount of funding that comes
to us from the, um, entertainment industry.
We also have fixed statutory obligations we have to, to do.
And that includes classifying films and shows
and that type of thing where we are, um,
what we are doing is shifting the balance of the work
that we were doing in that domain, making sure
that we can still do that work, but with much less time
and point it towards the forensic ification work
and the prevention and education.
So, I mean, I I I very much talk about this
as a public health issue.
Um, and we don't have all of the interventions available
to us or mandate to do it,
but we do have two very important mandates and law.
One is to classify the content
and one is to prevent, um, harm
by educating people on their rights and responsibilities.
Classifying the content is really important.
It's not a whack-a-mole exercise.
There is a lot of it out there,
but the more we classify, the more likely it is
that we can short circuit in future investigations,
particularly if, uh, law enforcement can just consult us
and say, yeah, we've already done it.
Um, and it, uh, is more likely
that internet services will take it down if they know
that it has a legal classification.
And we have evidence of that.
We work with, um, organizations like Tech Against Terrorism,
these, these types of organizations.
We set up post, um, the Terror Attacks in Christchurch.
And they essentially work with the main, uh,
social media platforms
to alert them when they're holding terror content.
And they have a framework of sort
of like, this is high alert.
If we classify something as objectional,
it will immediately go to the top of the high alert.
They spray the platforms and the content gets taken down,
and that can happen within hours.
Um, so that's a very tangible benefit
of doing more of, of this.
Yeah. So I guess the, to drill into
that question a little more, the,
what are the specific types of content
or what are, who are the specific audiences
that you think are the most at risk currently?
That's a great question. So the classification
Act is built on reducing risk of harm to the public.
And it is kind of, it gives us the power to reduce harm
to specific groups by restricting content by age.
Um, and so for decades we've been classifying films, uh,
because there is a kind of understanding that
that content is not appropriate.
Um, the objectionable content's not appropriate for anyone.
It is objectionable. And, um, if people aren't aware of
what types of content that is, that is,
and that's what we have found.
The New Zealand public are a little bit, um, shady on that.
We've answered that with this is what it is.
It is child sexual abuse material.
It is terror related material. It is bestiality material.
It is extreme violence and cruelty.
It is extreme sexual violence.
So that type of content, by nature
and by definition in the act is harmful.
Um, there, aside from classifying films
and, uh, giving extensive guidance to parents,
um, and whanau and teachers
and people who work with young people about other types
of harm that aren't illegal, we don't have a mandate to,
to classify the sort of sub illegal content online.
But I mean, we do lean into that mandate, our,
our information and education mandate.
So, um, our website, uh, is very popular with parents
who wanna understand, what is this game my kids are playing?
What do I need to know about it?
Um, how can I turn on the parental controls?
So I can't classify the game,
but I can inform people so
that they can make their own choices about it.
Um, yeah, supplementary, uh, yeah.
Or in your primary if you want. Oh, do you,
My, my, my question is why can't you classify the
Game? Uh,
so we can classify?
Uh, so game distributors are obliged
to get their game classified

In New Zealand, there is a, nobody really does that anymore.

Most people buy their games online these days,

I would have to call every single one in,

Uh, sometimes we do call them in if
we think they might be objectionable.
And, uh, in the last year we have banned a couple

Um, but the, the act does not, um,
require online games to be classified.
And, um, 'cause I wanted to ask and, um, drift in between.

So we work with eight provi streaming providers,

could you be classifying apps

and I understand that, and,

It's not that it's too hard, it's
that we don't have a mandate to classify things

So a lot of the, in fact, most

from online, but we're not classifying the website

Sometimes it might be a screenshot of the website if that's

Um, but often it's the image

And that is really important distinction
because let's say it's a, um, uh, a one minute video
of something abhor, uh, classifying that
and giving it a digital DNA means
that law enforcement can go after it on any website.
So the content is really,
the publication itself is really the most important thing
that our function can contribute
To. And, and, and
I guess the bit I'm getting to in terms

So, you know, if we think about ones

Thank you. So

I, why is that?
The law doesn't allow it.

if, if it's only online.
So it's not 'cause that there's seen to be any distinction

It's a known gap. Yeah. Cool.
I just have a supplementary question on that too.
The, so you've talked about the restrictions

or you could do, um,

Um, well, firstly, um, as a regulator, and
because we have quasi-judicial functions

But we, uh, participating, um, actively in all

And what we contribute is all of our research,

with young people about their experiences, uh, we've, uh,
participated in the select committees inquiry into
online harms, for instance.
Um, what I would say is that
we see minimizing, preventing responding to content harm

Um, the levers that we have are beautiful.
The ability to classify extremely harmful pieces
of content aid in criminal investigations, um,
potentially get it taken down.
And the mandate to educate and inform.
There needs to be a middle bit if, if that is so desired by,
by the, by the government, that it puts those guardrails in
to stop people getting in the door if

But the, the functions at the other end
of the system will still be needed.
Um, so we haven't advocated

In fact, what we have advocated for, if anything, is
to loosen up probably some
of the restrictions on the entertainment films and movies
and things that we do there.
We've demonstrated over the last five years

Um, we can probably put our expertise

Do you foresee a point where actually you need
to increase your personnel resources to meet continuing up
to of harmful contents?
Yes. I mean, there's a limit to how much we can do
and it is people, so the new technology will invest in,
hopefully make their jobs a lot easier.
The technology that they're working with now is
coming up 20 years old.
Um, and it just doesn't work for the volumes that we've got.
So, you know, that will make their job easier
and actually take a lot of the content, um, time
with the content, which is horrible stuff.
And I do need to emphasize that we're investing in it
for efficiency, but also to protect the staff
who shouldn't need to look at it 600
times and write a whole lot of stuff.
Um, but there will be a limit that we will reach.
And do you have any idea of when that will happen?
Well, we keep, um, uh, beating our own targets,
but I, I don't know, it will be in the coming years.
Um, and obviously costs for the office continue to increase
re uh, rents and salaries and those types of things.
Um, we recently as a board though,
commissioned New Zealand Institute of Economic Research
to do, um, cost benefit analysis just to make sure
that this is the right thing to be doing.
And, um, we can share the report with the committee,
but at a very conservative estimate, it is a one
to 1.1 return,
but it on a more, um, realistic estimate,
it's every dollar spent returns $2.20 to the community.
So it is a, it's, it's worth investing in.
Um, while we have a fixed budget, we just yeah,
shift the balance of our investment.
And do you use AI for classification?
We don't, um, for a couple of reasons.
Uh, these are legally binding decisions
and someone could go to prison
for 14 years off the back of them.
They're always going to need a human eye on that.
Uh, however, some of the CVO providers might
and if it's generating accurate results against
framework that we expect.
Thank you. We have now actually run outta time.
We, um, grateful to be, uh, for you to have you
with us today and also our really interesting discussion
and we didn't get to touch much on the staff wellbeing,
but I think we can understand from the committee that,
that we, um, are concerned, um, about the staff
and what they have to subject themselves to.
So good to hear that you're taking
that really seriously in the work.
And thank you again for the work that you do.
Yeah, thank you for.
